name: Roswell Incident 1947
description: The Roswell Incident refers to the purported crash of an unidentified flying object (UFO) near Roswell, New Mexico, in July 1947. Proponents believe the object was an extraterrestrial spacecraft, and that the U.S. government subsequently recovered the wreckage and any alien occupants, initiating a decades-long cover-up. The initial report was of a crashed 'flying disc' on a local ranch. Over time, the narrative evolved to include claims of recovered alien bodies and secret government facilities dedicated to reverse-engineering alien technology. Key evidence cited by proponents includes eyewitness accounts of the crash debris, alleged testimonies from military personnel involved in the recovery, and purported leaked documents. Mainstream explanation attributes the debris to Project Mogul, a top-secret U.S. Air Force project involving high-altitude balloons designed to detect Soviet nuclear tests. The supposed alien bodies are attributed to crash test dummies used in high-altitude parachute experiments during the 1950s and 1960s, which were later conflated with the 1947 event. Skeptics also point to the inconsistencies in eyewitness accounts over time and the lack of verifiable physical evidence supporting the extraterrestrial claim. The incident has become a cornerstone of UFO lore and a cultural phenomenon, fueled by popular books, movies, and television shows.
subtitle: Alleged UFO crash and cover-up.

claims.claim: A UFO crashed near Roswell in 1947.
claims.evidence: Eyewitness accounts, recovered debris (allegedly not from weather balloon).
claims.criticism: Debris identified as Project Mogul balloon remnants; eyewitness accounts inconsistent and evolving over time.

claims.claim: The U.S. government recovered alien bodies from the crash site.
claims.evidence: Alleged testimonies from military personnel, purported leaked documents detailing autopsy reports.
claims.criticism: No verifiable physical evidence of alien bodies; testimonies often lack corroboration; 'leaked' documents frequently debunked as hoaxes.

claims.claim: The government is engaged in a cover-up to hide the truth about the Roswell incident and extraterrestrial contact.
claims.evidence: Secrecy surrounding the incident, changing official explanations, alleged witness intimidation.
claims.criticism: Government secrecy can be attributed to national security concerns during the Cold War; changing explanations reflect evolving understanding of the event; no credible evidence of systematic witness intimidation.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel
keyFigures.role: Intelligence officer who initially investigated the debris field

keyFigures.name: Philip J. Klass
keyFigures.role: Skeptical investigator and debunker of UFO claims

timeline.event: Alleged UFO crash near Roswell, New Mexico.
Stanton Friedman interviews Jesse Marcel, reigniting interest in the Roswell incident.
U.S. Air Force releases report identifying the debris as from Project Mogul.
U.S. Air Force releases second report explaining 'alien bodies' as crash test dummies.

sources.author: Stanton Friedman & Don Berliner
sources.work: Crash at Corona: The Definitive Study of the Roswell Incident
sources.title: Stanton Friedman & Don Berliner

sources.author: Kal Korff
sources.work: The Roswell UFO Crash: What Really Happened
